sentiment.rationale: Cyrus proclaims the decree to rebuild the temple, attributing the charge to the LORD, and the people rise with generous offerings.
A census of those returning from exile, recording families, priests, Levites, and servants in orderly restoration.
The altar is rebuilt, sacrifices resume, and the temple foundation is laid amid shouts of praise and tears of the elders.
Adversaries oppose the rebuilding through letters to the Persian king, and the work is forcibly halted.
The prophets Haggai and Zechariah encourage rebuilding; the governor investigates but allows work to continue pending royal review.
Darius confirms Cyrus's decree, the temple is completed, and the people celebrate the dedication and Passover with great joy.
Ezra the scribe is introduced with his priestly lineage; Artaxerxes grants him authority and resources to beautify the temple.
Ezra assembles returnees, fasts and prays for safe travel rather than requesting a military escort, and they arrive safely in Jerusalem.
Ezra learns of intermarriage with foreign peoples, tears his garment, and offers a deeply sorrowful confession before God.
The people weep and covenant to put away foreign wives; a systematic process of repentance and separation is carried out.

missingCharacters.name: Shecaniah
missingCharacters.description: Son of Jehiel, who urged Ezra to put away the foreign wives, expressing hope for Israel's repentance.

missingCharacters.name: Tattenai
missingCharacters.description: Persian governor of the province beyond the river who inquired about the authority to rebuild the Jerusalem temple.

summaries.summary: King Cyrus of Persia issues a decree permitting the Jews to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the temple, and the people respond with generous offerings of gold, silver, and goods.
A detailed census of the families, priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, and temple servants who return from Babylonian captivity to Jerusalem.
The returned exiles rebuild the altar, reinstitute sacrifices and feast days, and lay the foundation of the temple amid mixed reactions of shouting and weeping.
Local adversaries attempt to join and then sabotage the rebuilding effort, eventually persuading the Persian court to halt construction of the temple.
Encouraged by the prophets Haggai and Zechariah, Zerubbabel and Jeshua resume building; governor Tattenai investigates and writes to Darius for confirmation.
Darius finds Cyrus's original decree, orders the temple work to continue with royal support, and the Jews complete and dedicate the temple with joyful celebration and Passover.
Ezra the scribe and priest is introduced with his lineage; King Artaxerxes commissions him with a letter granting authority and resources to go up to Jerusalem.
Ezra assembles the returning exiles by family, proclaims a fast for divine protection on the journey, and they arrive safely in Jerusalem with the temple treasures.
Ezra is horrified to learn that the people, priests, and Levites have intermarried with surrounding nations, and he offers a heartfelt prayer of confession and shame before God.
The people confess their sin of intermarriage, covenant to put away foreign wives, and a formal process is established to investigate and resolve each case.

crossReferences.note: Cyrus's decree fulfills Jeremiah's prophecy that after seventy years God would cause the return to Jerusalem.
Cyrus named by Isaiah as the shepherd who would decree the rebuilding of the temple.
The people sing praises echoing the refrain that the mercy of the LORD endures forever.
Parallels the joyful celebration of Passover with the great assembly in Hezekiah's time.
References the wall and restoration of Jerusalem, connecting to Nehemiah's later rebuilding effort.

verses.reason: Cyrus's proclamation inviting the Jews to return and rebuild the house of the LORD in Jerusalem.
The people praised the LORD with a great shout when the temple foundation was laid.
The elders who remembered Solomon's temple wept aloud, while others shouted for joy.
The LORD made them joyful and turned the heart of the king of Assyria unto them.
Ezra had prepared his heart to seek the law of the LORD, and to do it, and to teach statutes and judgments.
Ezra was ashamed to ask the king for soldiers because he had told the king God's hand is upon all who seek him.
Ezra's confession: our iniquities are increased over our head and our trespass is grown up unto the heavens.
Ezra praying and weeping before the house of God while a great congregation gathers weeping.
